symbols: arms raised triumphantly — Christ's victory over death, risen and glorified
skull at the base — death conquered and trampled underfoot
swirling angels behind — the heavenly host witnessing the greatest miracle in history
scarred bare torso — the wounds of the crucifixion now transformed into marks of glory
description: An expressionist composition showing a bare-chested figure at center with both arms raised high in a gesture of triumph. The figure has a pale body with visible markings or scars. At the base, a human skull rests. Behind the figure, swirling abstract forms in blue, gold, red, and circular patterns suggest angelic beings or cosmic forces. The overall palette is dark blue, ochre, and warm flesh tones. The style is bold and angular with distorted forms and intense color.
theological_description: Dix's expressionist Resurrection strips the event to its raw theological power. The risen Christ raises his arms in victory — 'Death has been swallowed up in victory' (1 Corinthians 15:54). The skull beneath his feet fulfills the promise of Genesis 3:15: the seed of the woman has crushed the serpent's head, and death itself is defeated. The visible wounds on the risen body are theologically crucial — the risen Christ is the same crucified Christ, his wounds now glorified (John 20:27). The swirling cosmic background suggests that the Resurrection is not merely a personal event but a transformation of all reality — 'if anyone is in Christ, the new creation has come' (2 Corinthians 5:17). Paul stakes everything on this event: 'If Christ has not been raised, your faith is futile' (1 Corinthians 15:17). The expressionist distortion conveys what no realistic painting can — the sheer strangeness and world-shattering power of a man returning from death.

scripture_refs.reason: Death swallowed up in victory — the triumphant gesture of the risen Christ
The risen Christ showing his wounds — the scarred body that has overcome death
He will crush your head — the skull under Christ's feet represents death conquered
New creation in Christ — the cosmic transformation suggested by the swirling background
